summary.brief: Slater v. Ichtertz. Medical malpractice case involving neurology.

evidence_index.text: In 2017, Dr. Dolf Ichtertz diagnosed Glen Slater with severe cubital tunnel syndrome, a condition that occurs when the ulnar nerve is compressed or entrapped at the elbow.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ichtertz recommended that Slater undergo a surgical procedure the parties describe as an 'endoscopic cubital tunnel release.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is undisputed that while Ichtertz was attempting to advance the guide proximally along Slater’s ulnar nerve to get it into position, he encountered resistance.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He described 'struggling' with the guide for a few seconds and stated that when he looked through the scope to identify what was causing the resistance, he saw that Slater’s ulnar nerve was 'bunching up' on the front end of the guide.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ichtertz removed the guide and discovered that a single fascicle of Slater’s ulnar nerve had been transected, or 'cut in half,' but was not displaced.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ichtertz repaired the fascicle using sutures and completed the cubital tunnel release procedure using different instruments.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Wysocki testified that in his opinion, Ichtertz breached the applicable standard of care by using the surgical instrument to apply a significant amount of force directly to the ulnar nerve, resulting in severe ulnar nerve injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Robert Wysocki

evidence_index.text: On this record, it was error to grant a directed verdict in favor of Ichtertz at the close of the Slaters’ case in chief.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Because we conclude the directed verdict should not have been granted on this record, we reverse the judgment and remand the cause for a new trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient diagnosed with severe cubital tunnel syndrome, a condition that occurs when the ulnar nerve is compressed or entrapped at the elbow.

requestables.name: Orthopedic Surgery Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation for surgical intervention for cubital tunnel syndrome

reveal.decision_impact: Leads to decision to perform endoscopic cubital tunnel release

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with symptoms of cubital tunnel syndrome

key_events.event: Diagnosis of severe cubital tunnel syndrome
Surgical guide advanced with excessive force

decision_points.clinical_context: During endoscopic cubital tunnel release surgery
decision_points.prompt: How should the surgical guide be advanced along the ulnar nerve?

options.description: Advance the guide with gentle pressure based on feel
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires avoiding excessive force to prevent nerve injury

options.description: Advance the guide with force to overcome resistance
options.clinical_reasoning: Excessive force can lead to nerve injury

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant used excessive force, resulting in nerve injury
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant struggled with the guide and applied force

expected_action_court.description: Use gentle pressure to avoid nerve injury
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony established the need for gentle handling

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Excessive force led to nerve transection
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Gentle pressure should have been used
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Ulnar nerve injury with transection of a fascicle
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Impairment to the left upper extremity

key_findings: Directed verdict in favor of Ichtertz was reversed

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to apply gentle pressure during guide positioning
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Gentle pressure would have prevented nerve injury

Excessive pressure applied during guide positioning
